summary:
The Treaty of Ryswick (September 20, 1697 AD) ended the [[event:3504|Nine Years' War]] between France and the Grand Alliance (England, the Netherlands, the Holy Roman Empire, and Spain) — one of the most significant peace treaties of the late 17th century, marking the first major check on [[event:2361|Louis XIV's]] expansionist ambitions and establishing a new equilibrium in European politics. The treaty required France to return most territorial acquisitions since 1679, recognize William III as King of England (previously Louis had supported the deposed Catholic James II), and restore Dutch commercial privileges. Louis XIV's acceptance of these terms, after 25 years of expansion that had alarmed all his neighbors, represented a significant diplomatic retreat.

The Treaty of Ryswick was significant for what it anticipated as well as what it resolved. The looming succession crisis in Spain — Louis XIV's relative Charles II of Spain was dying without an heir — made peace necessary to position France diplomatically for the coming contest. The question of who would inherit Spain's enormous empire would soon trigger the [[event:2347|War of the Spanish Succession]], the most destructive European conflict between the Thirty Years' War and the French Revolutionary Wars. In colonial matters, the treaty confirmed French possession of the western third of Hispaniola (modern Haiti), which had been a base for [[event:3277|Caribbean pirates]] and would develop into Saint-Domingue — France's most profitable colony and the future site of the [[event:2335|Haitian Revolution]]. Ryswick also formally recognized the principle that colonial possessions would be settled as part of European peace negotiations, establishing a pattern of colonial bargaining that would characterize 18th-century diplomacy.
